one_line_takeaway: BIRA shows, both theoretically (exponential decay in detection probability with per-token green-probability suppression) and empirically (>99% attack success across 7 diverse watermarking schemes and 3 LLM backbones), that a simple black-box, query-free rewriting attack using a self-information-guided negative logit bias can defeat current green/red-list and related LLM watermarks while preserving semantic fidelity substantially better than prior query-free attacks (DIPPER, SIRA).
key_finding: BIRA achieves >99% ASR essentially uniformly across all 7 watermarking schemes and all 3 rewriting backbones tested (Table 1), e.g., BIRA (Llama-3.1-8B) reaches 99.8% on KGW, 99.4% on Unigram, 99.8% on UPV, 100.0% on EWD, 100.0% on DIP, 99.6% on SIR, 99.8% on EXP -- versus the strongest prior baseline SIRA (Llama-3.1-8B) at 98.8/95.0/87.6/99.8/99.6/72.8/95.2% respectively, and Vanilla paraphrasing (GPT-4o-mini) averaging only 49.6% ASR versus BIRA's 99.5% average on the same backbone. On the SIR scheme specifically (SIRA's weakest case at 72.8-60.6% ASR), BIRA still hits 98.8-99.6%. z-score analysis (Table 4) shows BIRA drives the watermark statistic below the SIR threshold (tau=0.2) to -0.06 +/- 0.09 and below the Unigram threshold (tau=4.0) to -0.34 +/- 1.61, i.e. into the null-hypothesis range, versus SIRA's 0.14 +/- 0.12 (SIR) and 1.63 +/- 1.38 (Unigram), which remain closer to/above threshold. On 600-token long text from Qwen2.5-32B (Table 3), BIRA holds TPR@1%FPR to 4.0% with LLM-judge score 4.14, while SIRA degrades to TPR 20.8% and LLM-judge 1.33 (severe semantic collapse from its masking procedure at longer lengths). Ablations (Table 2) confirm the theoretical prediction: as |beta| increases from 0 to -9, ASR rises monotonically from 54.2% to ~99.6-99.8% while LLM-judgment score falls from 4.76 to 2.94 and perplexity rises from 9.17 to 14.10, exposing an explicit ASR/fidelity tradeoff; percentile q sweep shows q=0 (suppress all tokens) gives high ASR (99.0%) but worse fidelity, while q=0.9 (suppress almost nothing) drops ASR to 77.2% with best fidelity. Detection-bound analysis (Figure 5) on the Unigram scheme shows BIRA's empirical per-sample detection-probability upper bound (median 6.96e-3, P90 7.50e-2) is far below Vanilla's (median 2.23e-1, P90 7.97e-1), and self-information-guided token selection beats random token selection of the same size at every selection ratio tested (Figure 6).
core_contribution: The paper (1) derives a theoretical sufficient condition for defeating green/red-list LLM watermark detectors: if a rewriting attacker can keep the average conditional probability of sampling a 'green' token at least delta below the detector's decision threshold, the probability of the watermark being detected decays exponentially in delta^2 (proved via Azuma-Hoeffding on a martingale difference sequence, Lemma 4.1 + Theorem 4.2); and (2) turns this insight into a practical, query-free, black-box attack called Bias-Inversion Rewriting Attack (BIRA). BIRA identifies a 'proxy suppression set' of high-token-surprisal (self-information) tokens in the watermarked text under a public LLM, then rewrites the text while applying a negative logit bias to that proxy set at every decoding step, with an adaptive scheme that backs off the bias magnitude if a distinct-1-gram degeneration check fails.
threat_model: Black-box, query-free evasion: the adversary has no access to the watermarked model, no knowledge of the watermarking scheme W or its secret key k, and cannot query the watermarked model or its detector. The adversary only observes the watermarked text itself and uses an independent, unwatermarked public LLM (Llama-3.1-8B/70B or GPT-4o-mini) to rewrite it, with the goal of minimizing the detector's true-positive/detection probability subject to a semantic-similarity constraint on the rewrite relative to the original watermarked text (Eq. 1). This is explicitly contrasted with query-based attacks (e.g., Jovanovic et al. 2024; Chen et al. 2024; Wu & Chandrasekaran 2024) that require repeated access to the watermarked model to infer the green-token set -- those are described as prior/related work, not the paper's own method.
limitations: The paper's own scope notes: (1) the theoretical analysis (Lemma 4.1/Theorem 4.2) applies to detectors whose statistic is a nondecreasing function of the empirical green-token rate (e.g., the one-proportion z-test), so it is derived for green/red-list-style watermarks though the empirical attack is also shown to extend to EXP (sampling-based) and sentence-level watermarks; (2) degeneration under strong negative bias is real and only mitigated (not eliminated) by the adaptive backoff -- reported as occurring in 2.4% of 500 samples (average 1.03 iterations/text at lr=0.125), and Table 2 shows quality metrics degrade monotonically as |beta| grows, so the attack has an explicit efficacy/fidelity dial rather than a free lunch; (3) OPT-1.3B, the primary watermark-generation model, is noted by the authors as producing low-quality/high-entropy text, motivating a separate Qwen2.5-32B long-text validation; (4) GPT-4o-mini does not expose logits, requiring an auxiliary model (Llama-3.2-3B) plus re-tokenization workaround, whose robustness the authors check only in an appendix; (5) the paper explicitly frames its own release as dual-use in the Impact Statement, acknowledging the method could be used to bypass detection of misused AI content, and positions the work as red-teaming intended to spur more robust watermark defenses and evaluation practices rather than as a production evasion tool.

datasets_used: C4 (Raffel et al. 2020) -- 500 test prompts, primary evaluation set
Dolly CW (Conover et al. 2023) -- secondary evaluation, appendix
quantitative_results: ASR Table 1: SIRA (Llama-3.1-8B, prior SOTA baseline) on same 7 watermarks = 98.8%/95.0%/87.6%/99.8%/99.6%/72.8%/95.2%
ASR Table 1: Vanilla (GPT-4o-mini) average 49.6% vs BIRA (GPT-4o-mini) average 99.5%
z-score Table 4: BIRA on SIR (tau=0.2): -0.06 +/- 0.09; on Unigram (tau=4.0): -0.34 +/- 1.61 -- both below/near null; SIRA: 0.14 +/- 0.12 (SIR), 1.63 +/- 1.38 (Unigram)
Long-text Table 3 (600-token, Qwen2.5-32B, SIR watermark): BIRA TPR@1%FPR=4.0%, LLM-judge=4.14, PPL=8.90; SIRA TPR@1%FPR=20.8%, LLM-judge=1.33, PPL=7.19

Ablation Table 2 (beta sweep, q=0.5 fixed): ASR rises 54.2% (beta=0) to 99.6-99.8% (beta<=-4); LLM-judgment falls 4.76->2.94; Self-BLEU falls 0.25->0.01; Perplexity rises 9.17->14.10; Iterations rise 1.00->3.36
Ablation Table 2 (q sweep, beta=-4.0 fixed): ASR ranges 99.0% (q=0) to 77.2% (q=0.9); LLM-judgment 4.15->4.60; Self-BLEU 0.06->0.18
Detection-bound analysis (Unigram, Fig.5): BIRA median upper bound 6.96e-3, P90 7.50e-2; Vanilla median 2.23e-1, P90 7.97e-1
Degeneration rate: 2.4% over 500 samples, average 1.03 iterations/text at lr=0.125
Worked KGW numeric example in Section 4.1: default p0=0.5, tau=4, N=230 gives p_tau≈0.632; delta=0.1 -> detection bound e^-1.15≈0.316; delta=0.2 -> e^-4.6≈0.010
baselines_compared: DIPPER-1 and DIPPER-2 (Krishna et al. 2023, trained paraphrasing model at two diversity settings)
SIRA (Cheng et al. 2025) -- prior state-of-the-art query-free masking-and-regeneration attack
Adaptive query-based attack (Diaa et al. 2024, assumes knowledge of watermarking scheme) -- compared in appendix
